pages.text: The skeleton of a building: the pitched roof above, the stairs within, the bridges between, and the awnings that overhang the door. From $(italic)Macaw's Roofs$(), $(italic)Macaw's Stairs$(), $(italic)Macaw's Bridges$(), and $(italic)Another Furniture$().
pages.title: Roofs & Stairs

pages.text: $(bold)Pitched roofs.$() Proper $(italic)pitched$() roofs in every vanilla material — brick, slate, stone, terracotta, wood — with edge pieces, corner pieces, internal intersections, attic windows, and rain gutters. The day you place your first one is the day your buildings stop looking like cobblestone wedges.$(br2)Slope steepness comes in three pitches: shallow, standard, and steep.
pages.title: Roofs

pages.text: $(bold)Stairs, handrails, balconies.$() Proper $(bold)handrails$() and banisters that attach to vanilla and modded stairs alike — place a stair, then place a railing on either side, and it follows the slope. $(bold)Balconies$() overhang walls without supports, the way real balconies do.
pages.title: Stairs

pages.text: $(bold)Awnings.$() $(italic)Another Furniture$() striped-fabric awnings in 16 dyed colours, sized to span a doorway. Hung above a Macaw's door, they soften the entrance the way a real shop's canopy does.
pages.title: Awnings

pages.text: $(bold)Bridges.$() Rope bridges that sway between cliffs; plank bridges with railings; stone bridges that arch over rivers; japanese arched bridges for the garden. Each comes with matching support posts, railings, and end caps. A river in Novus no longer needs a dirt-causeway crossing.
pages.title: Bridges
